policy_statement: Italy increases the use of coal in power plants from 9% to 14% (as part of measures already adopted under the trend scenario).
mechanism_description: It increases coal use in power plants, which raises the carbon intensity of electricity generation and increases power-sector greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel switch (toward coal) → ↑ combustion CO₂ emissions per kWh → ↑ total power-sector GHG emissions → ↑ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The electricity supply mix shifts toward greater coal generation (higher coal capacity utilization and/or more coal capacity and dispatch).  
(2) Mediating physical process: Burning coal oxidizes carbon to CO₂ (and emits CH₄/N₂O in smaller quantities) at a higher emissions intensity per unit electricity than most alternatives; upstream coal mining/transport can add methane emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Higher lifecycle GHG emissions from the power sector (especially CO₂), worsening mitigation performance and increasing radiative forcing relative to a lower-coal baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: Increasing coal-fired electricity generation raises power-sector CO₂ emissions, increasing net greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: More coal burned for power → higher CO₂ per kWh and typically higher total CO₂ → increased net GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The single largest climate-relevant effect is the change in GHG emissions (mitigation domain); it is not primarily about reducing energy input (resource efficiency), reducing hazard exposure (adaptation), or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Even if coal displaces oil/diesel generation, coal usually remains high-carbon and the mechanism’s defining feature is increased coal use; any local air-quality co-benefits/harms or reliability benefits do not change the dominant climate pathway. CCS would be a different mechanism (coal + capture), not this one.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that increases coal generation or coal share in electricity supply will, in expectation and in the dominant causal chain, increase power-sector GHG emissions relative to a counterfactual with less coal.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., “negative mitigation / emissions increase,” “fossil fuel expansion,” “energy security”) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on the specific design and context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If coal replaces an even more carbon-intensive temporary backup (rare) or if fully coupled with high capture rates CCS, the net effect could differ; those cases should be represented by different canonical mechanisms (e.g., “coal_with_CCS → emissions_reduction” or “diesel_to_coal → emissions_change”). Otherwise straightforward.
key_indicators: "increase in the use of coal in power plants"; "from 9% to 14%"
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument is not explicitly stated (could reflect multiple underlying tools such as subsidies, dispatch rules, or delayed phase-out), so classification is inferred as mix/usage target rather than a clearly specified regulatory or fiscal instrument.
